en.extract:
Clark's nutcracker (Nucifraga columbiana), also known as Clark's crow, is a passerine bird in the family Corvidae, native to the mountains of western North America. The nutcracker is an omnivore, but subsists mainly on pine nuts, burying seeds in the ground in the summer and then retrieving them in the winter by memory.
The bird was described by the Lewis and Clark Expedition, with William Clark first observing it in 1805 along the banks of the Salmon River, a tributary of the Columbia River.


Etymology and history
In William Clark's initial description of the bird, he mistakenly described it as a woodpecker, writing "I saw today [a] bird of the woodpecker kind, which fed on pine burrs. Its bill and tail white; the wings black; every other part of a light brown, and about the size of a robin."

Clark's nutcracker's scientific name literally means "nutcracker of the Columbia". In 1806, Meriwether Lewis recorded a more detailed description from specimens collected along the Clearwater River in Idaho. A skin collected by the expedition was obtained by the ornithologist Alexander Wilson, who used it to produce an engraving for his American Ornithology work.


Taxonomy
Originally placed in the genus Corvus by Wilson, by 1840 Clark's nutcracker was included in Nucifraga by John James Audubon. Through the second half of the 19th century, taxonomists placed Clark's nutcracker in its own genus, Picicorvus, as suggested by Charles Lucien Bonaparte. In 1894, ornithologist Robert Ridgway suggested its return to Nucifraga, citing morphological similarities to its modern Eurasian congenerics.


Distribution and habitat

This species is present in western North America from British Columbia and western Alberta in the north to Baja California and central New Mexico in the south. There is also a small isolated population on the peak of Cerro Potosí, elevation 3,700 metres (12,200 ft), in Nuevo León, northeast Mexico. It is mainly found in mountains at altitudes of 900–3,900 metres (3,000–12,900 ft) in conifer forest. It is not typically migratory, but does seasonally move between higher elevations in the summer breeding season and lower elevations in the winter. Some populations of Clark's nutcracker will remain year-round at high elevations if they have sufficient food caches. In years where pine mast production is particularly poor, Clark's nutcracker may leave its normal range and travel as far north as Alaska, and east as the Great Plains. Vagrants have occurred as far east as Pennsylvania and Ontario.


Description
Nucifraga columbiana is roughly the size of a jay, and slightly smaller than its Eurasian congenerics (northern, southern, and Kashmir nutcrackers). Its head, back, belly, and ventrum are covered in gray feathers, and it sports black wings with white tips on secondary feathers. Its tail feathers are easily distinguishable in flight, with central black feathers bordered by white feathers on either side. It has a long, black, pointed bill whose shape is specialized for removing the scales of pinecones.


Measurements
Length: 10.6–11.8 inches (27–30 cm)
Weight: 3.7–5.7 ounces (100–160 g)
Wingspan: 24 inches (61 cm)


Food

The most important food resources for this species are the seeds of pines (Pinus sp.), principally two cold-climate (high-altitude) species of white pine with large seeds: the whitebark pine (P. albicaulis) and limber pine (P. flexilis). Seeds of other high-altitude pine species are also eaten, including the foxtail pine (P. balfouriana), bristlecone pine (P. longaeva) western white pine (P. monticola), and ponderosa pine (P. ponderosa). Douglas fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) is one of the few non-pine conifers whose seeds Clark's nutcracker regularly eats. During migrations to lower altitudes, it also extensively uses the seeds of pinyon pines. The isolated Cerro Potosí population is strongly associated with the local endemic Potosi pinyon (Pinus culminicola). All Clark's nutcrackers have a sublingual pouch capable of holding around 50–150 seeds, depending on the size of the seeds, which helps the birds more easily transport seeds between caches.
Clark's nutcrackers store seeds, usually in the ground for later consumption, in caches of 1–15 seeds (average of 3–4 seeds). They favor cache sites on steep, south-facing mountain slopes, as snow will melt here earliest in the year. Depending on the cone crop as well as the tree species, a single Clark's nutcracker can cache as many as 98,000 seeds per season. The birds regularly store more than they actually need as insurance against seed theft by other animals (squirrels, etc.), as well as low availability of alternative foods; this surplus seed is left in the cache, and may be able to germinate and grow into new trees. This storage behavior allows demonstration of the bird's long-term spatial memory; they are able to relocate caches of seeds with great accuracy up to nine months after initial storage. Clark's nutcrackers are heavily dependent on food retrieved from caches throughout the winter, and will dig up caches, even when buried under heavy snow. Short-term pictoral memory tasks reveal that while nutcrackers have particularly adept spatial memories, their visual memory is unremarkable; the cognitive ability they use to recover caches appears to be specifically evolved for this purpose.

Clark's nutcrackers, like other Corvids, are opportunistic feeders whose diet also includes a wide range of insect prey, rodents, nestling birds and eggs, amphibians, and carrion. In rare cases, they may attack and eat adult birds. In backyard bird feeders, they will take peanuts and suet. Clark's nutcrackers will also eat unattended human food in developed areas, and are known to some as "camp robbers".


Nesting

The species usually nests in pines or other types of conifers during early spring. Two to six eggs are laid per nest, with incubation occurring over approximately 18 days. Incubation is performed by both the male and female parents, and both the male and the female develop brood patches; however, the female parent contributes significantly more time to incubation than the male. Nestlings fledge between 3–4 weeks after hatch.


Whitebark pine mutualism
Clark's nutcracker is the primary seed disperser for whitebark pine (Pinus albicaulis). Whitebark pine is in decline throughout its range, due to infection by white pine blister rust (Cronartium ribicola), widespread outbreaks of mountain pine beetle, and the long-term effects of fire suppression. Clark's nutcracker is an integral part of the whitebark pine restoration process: Clark's nutcracker must remain in whitebark pine forests and cache the seeds in excess, so that healthy trees will continue to grow. If whitebark pine declines into extinction, Clark's nutcracker will lose an important source of food and may no longer be seen in areas where the tree is the primary source of seed, such as Glacier National Park.


Vocalization
Corvids, of which Clark's nutcracker is a member, do not typically have songs. However, they produce between eight and thirteen variable calls, generally phonetically described as "kraak" or "kraa".  Additional clicking or crackling calls are made, likely used for pair bonding.


References


External links

Clark's nutcracker on the Internet Bird Collection
Clark's Nutcracker at National Park Service

''Picicorvus columbianus'' Bonaparte, 1850 }} '''Clark's nutcracker''' ('''''Nucifraga columbiana'''''), also known as '''Clark's crow''', is a [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Corvidae]], native to the mountains of western North America. The nutcracker is an omnivore, but subsists mainly on [[pine nut]]s, burying seeds in the ground in the summer and then retrieving them in the winter by memory. The bird was described by the [[Lewis and Clark Expedition]], with [[William Clark (explorer)|William Clark]] first observing it in 1805 along the banks of the [[Salmon River (Washington)|Salmon River]], a tributary of the [[Columbia River]]. ==Etymology and history== In William Clark's initial description of the bird, he mistakenly described it as a woodpecker, writing "I saw today [a] bird of the woodpecker kind, which fed on pine burrs. Its bill and tail white; the wings black; every other part of a light brown, and about the size of a robin."{{Cite web |title=August 22, 1805 {{!}} Journals of the Lewis and Clark Expedition |url=https://lewisandclarkjournals.unl.edu/item/lc.jrn.1805-08-22#lc.jrn.1805-08-22.02 |access-date=2025-10-29 |website=lewisandclarkjournals.unl.edu}} [[File:Drawing of Clark's nutcracker by Alexander Wilson.png|thumb|left|upright|Original illustration of Clark's nutcracker by [[Alexander Wilson (ornithologist)|Alexander Wilson]]]] Clark's nutcracker's scientific name literally means "nutcracker of the Columbia". In 1806, [[Meriwether Lewis]] recorded a more detailed description from specimens collected along the [[Clearwater River (Idaho)|Clearwater River]] in Idaho.{{Cite web |title=May 28, 1806 {{!}} Journals of the Lewis and Clark Expedition |url=https://lewisandclarkjournals.unl.edu/item/lc.jrn.1806-05-28#ln35052802 |access-date=2025-10-29 |website=lewisandclarkjournals.unl.edu}}{{Cite web |last=Mussulman |first=Joseph A. |date= 4 June 2021|title=Clark's Nutcracker |url=https://lewis-clark.org/sciences/birds/clarks-nutcracker/ |access-date=9 June 2025 |website=Discover Lewis & Clark}} A skin collected by the expedition was obtained by the ornithologist [[Alexander Wilson (ornithologist)|Alexander Wilson]], who used it to produce an engraving for his ''American Ornithology'' work. ==Taxonomy== Originally placed in the genus ''[[Corvus]]'' by Wilson,{{Cite book |last=Wilson |first=Alexander |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/175516 |title=American Ornithology |date=1811 |publisher=Bradford and Inskeep |volume=3 |location=Philadelphia, PA, USA |pages=29–30}}{{cite book|last=Bent|first=AC|year=1946|title=Life histories of North American jays, crows, and titmice: Order Passeriformes|publisher=U.S. National Museum|id=Bulletin 191}} by 1840 Clark's nutcracker was included in ''Nucifraga'' by [[John James Audubon]].{{Cite book |last1=Audubon |first1=John James |url=http://archive.org/details/birdsofamericafr04audu |title=The birds of America: from drawings made in the United States and their territories |last2=Bowen |first2=John T. |date=1840 |publisher=New York : Published by J.J. Audubon; Philadelphia : J.B. Chevalier |others=Smithsonian Libraries}} Through the second half of the 19th century, taxonomists placed Clark's nutcracker in its own genus, ''Picicorvus'', as suggested by [[Charles Lucien Bonaparte]].{{cite book|last=Bonaparte|first=CL|year=1850|title=Conspectus Generum Avium|volume=1|publisher=EJ Brill|location=Leiden, Netherlands}} In 1894, ornithologist Robert Ridgway suggested its return to ''Nucifraga'', citing morphological similarities to its modern Eurasian congenerics.{{cite journal|last=Ridgway|first=R|year=1894|title=Picicorvus an untenable genus|journal=The Auk|volume=11|issue=2|page=179|url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/auk/vol11/iss2/37}}{{cite book|last1=Schaming|first1=TD|first2=DF|last2=Tomback|first3=TJ|last3=Lorenz|chapter=Clark's Nutcracker (Nucifraga columbiana) |year=2024|title=Birds of the World|editor-first=ND|editor-last=Sly|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|location=Ithaca, NY, USA|doi=10.2173/bow.clanut.02}} ==Distribution and habitat== [[File:Nucifraga columbiana1.jpg|thumb|In [[Deschutes National Forest]]]] This species is present in western North America from [[British Columbia]] and western [[Alberta]] in the north to [[Baja California]] and central [[New Mexico]] in the south.{{Cite web |title=Clark's Nutcracker {{!}} Audubon Field Guide |url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/clarks-nutcracker |access-date=2025-06-09 |website=www.audubon.org |language=en}} There is also a small isolated population on the peak of [[Cerro Potosí]], elevation 3,700 metres (12,200 ft), in [[Nuevo León]], northeast [[Mexico]].{{Cite journal |last=Leopold |first=A. Starker |date=1946 |title=Clark Nutcracker in Nuevo Leon, Mexico |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/condor/vol48/iss6/10 |journal=Condor |volume=48 |issue=6 |page=10}}{{Cite journal |last=Contreras-Balderas |first=Armando J. |date=1992 |title=Status of Clark's nutcrackers on Cerro el Potosi, Nuevo Leon, Mexico |journal=Western Birds |volume=23 |page=181|url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/western_birds/vol23/iss4/11}} It is mainly found in mountains at altitudes of 900–3,900 metres (3,000–12,900 ft) in conifer forest.{{Cite web |title=Clark's Nutcracker Life History, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Clarks_Nutcracker/lifehistory |access-date=2025-06-09 |website=www.allaboutbirds.org |language=en}} It is not typically migratory, but does seasonally move between higher elevations in the summer breeding season and lower elevations in the winter.{{Cite Clark's Nutcrackers in the Cascade Range |url=https://research.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/34965 |journal=The Condor |volume=111 |issue=2 |pages=326-340 |doi=10.1525/cond.2009.080070}} In years where pine mast production is particularly poor, Clark's nutcracker may leave its normal range and travel as far north as Alaska, and east as the Great Plains. Vagrants have occurred as far east as Pennsylvania and Ontario. ==Description== ''Nucifraga columbiana'' is roughly the size of a [[jay]], and slightly smaller than its [[Nutcracker (bird)|Eurasian congenerics]] ([[Northern nutcracker|northern]], [[Southern nutcracker|southern]], and [[Kashmir nutcracker|Kashmir]] nutcrackers).{{cite book|last1=Kirwan|first1=GM|first2=S|last2=Madge|first3=J|last3=del Hoyo|first4=DA|last4=Christie|first5=N|last5=Collar|first6=N|last6=Moura|first7=PFD|last7=Boesman|first8=P|last8=Pyle|chapter=Southern Nutcracker (Nucifraga hemispila) |year=2024|title=Birds of the World|editor1-first=GM|editor1-last=Kirwan|editor2-first=BK|editor2-last=Keeney|editor3-first=MG|editor3-last=Smith|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|location=Ithaca, NY, USA|doi=10.2173/bow.eurnut4.01}}{{cite book|last1=Kirwan|first1=GM|first2=S|last2=Madge|first3=J|last3=del Hoyo|first4=DA|last4=Christie|first5=N|last5=Collar|first6=N|last6=Moura|first7=P|last7=Pyle|chapter=Northern Nutcracker (Nucifraga caryocatactes) |year=2024|title=Birds of the World|editor1-first=GM|editor1-last=Kirwan|editor2-first=BK|editor2-last=Keeney|editor3-first=MG|editor3-last=Smith|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|location=Ithaca, NY, USA|doi=10.2173/bow.eurnut3.01}}{{cite book|last1=Yousuf|first1=P|first2=AJ|last2=Spencer|year=2025|chapter=Kashmir Nutcracker (''Nucifraga multipunctata''), version 2.0|title=Birds of the World|editor1-first=ND|editor1-last=Sly|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|location=Ithaca, NY, USA|doi=10.2173/bow.sponut1.02}} Its head, back, belly, and ventrum are covered in gray feathers, and it sports black wings with white tips on secondary feathers. Its tail feathers are easily distinguishable in flight, with central black feathers bordered by white feathers on either side. It has a long, black, pointed bill whose shape is specialized for removing the scales of pinecones.{{Cite web |last=Grunbaum |first=Mara |date=2023 |title=As Stands of Whitebark Pine Disappear, Could the Clark's Nutcracker Soon Follow? |url=https://www.audubon.org/magazine/stands-whitebark-pine-disappear-could-clarks-nutcracker-soon-follow |access-date=9 June 2025 |website=Audubon Magazine}} ===Measurements=== * '''Length''': {{convert|10.6|-|11.8|in|cm}}{{Cite web|title=Clark's Nutcracker Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Clarks_Nutcracker/id|access-date=2020-09-27|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}{{cite web|url=https://www.nps.gov/romo/learn/nature/clarks_nutcracker.htm|title=Clark's Nutcracker|website=Rocky Mountain National Park|publisher=National Park Service|access-date=28 May 2023|date=20 May 2018}} * '''Weight''': {{convert|3.7|-|5.7|oz|g}} * '''Wingspan''': {{convert|24|in|cm}}{{Cite web|title=Clark's Nutcracker|url=https://www.eastsideaudubon.org/corvid-crier/2019/8/28/clarks-nutcracker|access-date=2020-09-27|website=Eastside Audubon Society|date=4 May 2018 |language=en-US}} ==Food== [[File: Clark's Nutcracker. Nucifraga columbiana - Flickr - gailhampshire.jpg|thumb|Clark's nutcracker feeding on seeds of pines]] The most important food resources for this species are the seeds of [[pine]]s (''Pinus'' sp.), principally two cold-climate (high-altitude) species of [[Pinus classification|white pine]] with large seeds: the whitebark pine (''[[Whitebark pine|P. albicaulis]]'') and limber pine (''[[Limber pine|P. flexilis]]''). Seeds of other high-altitude pine species are also eaten, including the foxtail pine (''[[Foxtail pine|P. balfouriana]]'')'','' bristlecone pine (''[[Bristlecone pine|P. longaeva]]'') western white pine (''[[Western white pine|P. monticola]]''), and ponderosa pine (''[[Pinus ponderosa|P. ponderosa]]'').{{Cite journal |last1=Giuntoli |first1=Mervin |last2=Mewaldt |first2=L. Richard |date=1978 |title=Stomach contents of Clark's nutcrackers collected in western Montana |journal=The Auk |volume=95 |issue=3 |pages=595–598|doi=10.1093/auk/95.3.595a |jstor=4085172 }} Douglas fir (''[[Douglas fir|Pseudotsuga menziesii]]'') is one of the few non-pine conifers whose seeds Clark's nutcracker regularly eats. During [[Altitudinal migration|migrations to lower altitudes]], it also extensively uses the seeds of [[pinyon pine]]s.{{Cite journal |last1=Vander Wall |first1=Stephen B. |last2=Balda |first2=Russell P. |date=1977 |title=Coadaptations of the Clark's nutcracker and the pinon pine for efficient seed harvest and dispersal |journal=Ecological Monographs |volume=47 |issue=1 |pages=89–111|doi=10.2307/1942225 |jstor=1942225 |bibcode=1977EcoM...47...89V }} The isolated Cerro Potosí population is strongly associated with the local endemic [[Potosi pinyon]] (''Pinus culminicola)''.{{Citation needed|date=June 2025}} All Clark's nutcrackers have a [[sublingual pouch]] capable of holding around 50–150 seeds, depending on the size of the seeds, which helps the birds more easily transport seeds between caches.{{cite book|last=Tomback|first=DF|author-link=Diana Tomback|year=1998|chapter=Clark's Nutcracker (''Nucifraga columbiana'')|editor1-first=A|editor1-last=Poole|editor2-first=F|editor2-last=Gill|title=The Birds of North America|number=331|publisher=The Birds of North America, Inc.|location=Philadelphia}} Clark's nutcrackers store seeds, usually in the ground for later consumption, in caches of 1–15 seeds (average of 3–4 seeds). They favor cache sites on steep, south-facing mountain slopes, as snow will melt here earliest in the year. Depending on the cone crop as well as the tree species, a single Clark's nutcracker can cache as many as 98,000 seeds per season.{{cite journal|last1=Hutchins|first1=HE|first2=FM|last2=Lanner|year=1982|title=The central role of Clark's nutcracker in the dispersal and establishment of whitebark pine|journal=Oecologia|volume=55|issue=2 |pages=192–201|doi=10.1007/BF00384487 |pmid=28311233 |bibcode=1982Oecol..55..192H |s2cid=16214504 }} The birds regularly store more than they actually need as insurance against seed theft by other animals ([[squirrel]]s, etc.), as well as low availability of alternative foods; this surplus seed is left in the cache, and may be able to germinate and grow into new trees. This storage behavior allows demonstration of the bird's long-term spatial memory; they are able to relocate caches of seeds with great accuracy up to nine months after initial storage.{{cite journal|last=Tomback|first=DF|author-link=Diana Tomback|year=1978|title=Foraging strategies of Clark's Nutcracker|journal=Living Bird|volume=16|pages=123–161}} Clark's nutcrackers are heavily dependent on food retrieved from caches throughout the winter, and will dig up caches, even when buried under heavy snow.{{Cite journal |last1=Balda |first1=Russell P. |last2=Kamil |first2=Alan |date=2006 |title=Linking Life Zones, Life History Traits, Ecology, and Spatial Cognition in Four Allopatric Southwestern Seed Caching Corvids |url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/bioscibehavior/36 |journal=Papers in Behavior and Biological Sciences}} Short-term pictoral memory tasks reveal that while nutcrackers have particularly adept spatial memories, their visual memory is unremarkable; the cognitive ability they use to recover caches appears to be specifically evolved for this purpose.{{Cite journal |last1=Qadri |first1=Muhammad A. J. |last2=Leonard |first2=Kevin |last3=Cook |first3=Robert G. |last4=Kelly |first4=Debbie M. |date=2018-12-01 |title=Examination of long-term visual memorization capacity in the Clark's nutcracker (Nucifraga columbiana) |journal=Psychonomic Bulletin & Review |language=en |volume=25 |issue=6 |pages=2274–2280 |doi=10.3758/s13423-018-1439-4 |issn=1531-5320 |jstor=1367199 |pmid=29450792 |doi-access=free}} [[File: Clark's Nutcracker with wings out, landing on a rock.JPG|thumb|left|Clark's nutcracker landing, [[Mount Hood]], Oregon]] Clark's nutcrackers, like other Corvids, are opportunistic feeders whose diet also includes a wide range of [[insect]] prey, rodents, nestling birds and eggs, amphibians, and carrion.{{Cite journal |last1=Mulder |first1=Barry S. |last2=Schultz |first2=Brian B. |last3=Sherman |first3=Paul W. |date=1978-12-01 |title=Predation on vertebrates by Clark's Nutcrackers |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/condor/vol80/iss4/19 |journal=Condor |language=en |volume=80 |issue=4 |pages=449–451 |doi=10.2307/1367199|jstor=1367199 }} In rare cases, they may attack and eat adult birds.{{Cite journal |last=MacCracken |first=Helen D. |date=1949 |title=White-winged Junco Killed by Clark's Nutcracker |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/auk/vol66/iss2/43 |journal=The Auk |volume=66 |issue=2}} In backyard bird feeders, they will take [[peanut]]s and suet. Clark's nutcrackers will also eat unattended human food in developed areas, and are known to some as "camp robbers". ==Nesting== [[File:Clark's Nutcracker at Crater Lake 2015.jpg|thumb|A Clark's nutcracker nestled on a branch at Crater Lake National Park in Oregon.]] The species usually nests in pines or other types of conifers during early spring. Two to six eggs are laid per nest, with incubation occurring over approximately 18 days. Incubation is performed by both the male and female parents, and both the male and the female develop [[brood patch]]es; however, the female parent contributes significantly more time to incubation than the male.{{Cite journal |last=Mewaldt |first=L. Richard |date=1956 |title=Nesting behavior of the Clark nutcracker |journal=The Condor |volume=58 |issue=1|pages=3–23 |doi=10.2307/1365043 |jstor=1365043 }} Nestlings fledge between 3–4 weeks after hatch.{{Cite journal |last=Schaming |first=Taza D. |date=2015 |title=Population-Wide Failure to Breed in the Clark's Nutcracker (Nucifraga columbiana) |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=10 |issue=5 |article-number=e0123917 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0123917|doi-access=free |pmid=25970294 |pmc=4430254 |bibcode=2015PLoSO..1023917S }} ==Whitebark pine mutualism== Clark's nutcracker is the primary seed disperser for [[Whitebark pine|whitebark pine (''Pinus albicaulis'')]].{{cite journal|last=Tomback|first=DF|author-link=Diana Tomback|year=1982|title=Dispersal of whitebark pine seeds by Clark's Nutcracker: A mutualism hypothesis|journal=Journal of Animal Ecology|volume=51|issue=2 |pages=451–467|doi=10.2307/3976 |jstor=3976 |bibcode=1982JAnEc..51..451T }} Whitebark pine is in decline throughout its range, due to infection by [[Cronartium ribicola|white pine blister rust (''Cronartium ribicola'')]], widespread outbreaks of [[mountain pine beetle]], and the long-term effects of fire suppression.{{cite book|last1=Tomback|first1=DF|author1-link=Diana Tomback|first2=SF|last2=Arno|first3=RE|last3=Keane|year=2001|title=Whitebark Pine Communities: Ecology and Restoration|publisher=Island Press|location=Washington, DC.}} Clark's nutcracker is an integral part of the whitebark pine restoration process: Clark's nutcracker must remain in whitebark pine forests and cache the seeds in excess, so that healthy trees will continue to grow. If whitebark pine declines into extinction, Clark's nutcracker will lose an important source of food and may no longer be seen in areas where the tree is the primary source of seed, such as [[Glacier National Park (U.S.)|Glacier National Park]].{{cite journal |last1=Ray |first1=Chris |last2=Rochefort |first2=Regina M. |last3=Ransom |first3=Jason I. |last4=Nesmith |first4=Jonathan C. B. |last5=Haultain |first5=Sylvia A. |last6=Schaming |first6=Taza D. |last7=Boetsch |first7=John R. |last8=Holmgren |first8=Mandy L. |last9=Wilkerson |first9=Robert L. |last10=Siegel |first10=Rodney B. |title=Assessing trends and vulnerabilities in the mutualism between whitebark pine (Pinus albicaulis) and Clark's nutcracker (Nucifraga columbiana) in national parks of the Sierra-Cascade region |journal=PLOS ONE |date=14 October 2020 |volume=15 |issue=10 |article-number=e0227161 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0227161 |pmid=33052936 |doi-access=free |pmc=7556478 |bibcode=2020PLoSO..1527161R }} ==Vocalization== [[Corvidae|Corvids]], of which Clark's nutcracker is a member, do not typically have songs. However, they produce between eight and thirteen variable calls, generally phonetically described as "''kraak''" or "''kraa''". Additional clicking or crackling calls are made, likely used for pair bonding. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Refbegin}} * {{cite book|last=Lanner|first=RM|year=1996|title=Made for each other: a symbiosis of birds and pines|publisher=OUP|isbn=0-19-508903-0}} * {{cite web|url=https://explorer.natureserve.org/Taxon/ELEMENT_GLOBAL.2.101001/Nucifraga_columbiana|title=Nucifraga columbiana|publisher=NatureServe|access-date=2025-06-09}} {{Refend}} ==External links== {{Commons and category|Nucifraga columbiana}} * [http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/clarks-nutcracker-nucifraga-columbiana Clark's nutcracker] on the Internet Bird Collection * [https://www.nps.gov/thingstodo/clark-s-nutcracker.htm Clark's Nutcracker] at National Park Service {{Corvidae|2}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q1142785}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Birds described in 1811]] [[Category:Birds of the Rio Grande valleys]] [[Category:Birds of the Sierra Nevada (United States)]] [[Category:Fauna of the Chihuahuan Desert]] [[Category:Native birds of the Western United States]] [[Category:Native birds of Western Canada]] [[Category:Nucifraga]] [[Category:Taxa named by Alexander Wilson (ornithologist)]]

zh.extract:
北美星鸦（学名：Nucifraga columbiana），是鸦科星鸦属的一种，分布于墨西哥、加拿大和美国。全球活动范围约为2,290,000平方千米。


进食习性
北美星鸦通常埋葬种子的方式贮藏食物。


体重
北美星鸦的平均体重约为130.0克。


栖息地
栖息地包括温带森林、城市、亚热带或热带的旱林和高海拔疏灌丛。


保护级别
该物种的保护状况被评为未濒危。


参考文献

Nucifraga columbiana. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-04）.
